From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

National anthem of Canada

This article is about the national anthem of Canada. For other uses, see [O Canada (disambiguation)](/wiki/O_Canada_(disambiguation) "O Canada (disambiguation)").

**This article contains [special characters](/wiki/Help:Special_characters "Help:Special characters").** Without proper [rendering support](/wiki/Help:Special_characters "Help:Special characters"), you may see [question marks, boxes, or other symbols](/wiki/Specials_(Unicode_block)#Replacement_character "Specials (Unicode block)").

"**O Canada**" ([French](/wiki/French_language "French language"): Ô Canada) is the [national anthem](/wiki/National_anthem "National anthem") of [Canada](/wiki/Canada "Canada"). The song was originally commissioned by [Lieutenant Governor of Quebec](/wiki/Lieutenant_Governor_of_Quebec "Lieutenant Governor of Quebec") [Théodore Robitaille](/wiki/Th%C3%A9odore_Robitaille "Théodore Robitaille") for the 1880 [Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day](/wiki/Saint-Jean-Baptiste_Day "Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day") ceremony; [Calixa Lavallée](/wiki/Calixa_Lavall%C3%A9e "Calixa Lavallée") composed the music, after which French-language words were written by the poet and judge Sir [Adolphe-Basile Routhier](/wiki/Adolphe-Basile_Routhier "Adolphe-Basile Routhier").

The original French lyrics were translated to English in 1906.[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1) Multiple English versions ensued, with [Robert Stanley Weir](/wiki/Robert_Stanley_Weir "Robert Stanley Weir")'s 1908 version (which was not a translation of the French lyrics) gaining the most popularity; the Weir lyrics eventually served as the basis for the official lyrics enacted by Parliament.[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1) Weir's English-language lyrics have been revised three times, most recently when *An Act to amend the National Anthem Act (gender)* was enacted in 2018.[[2]](#cite_note-BBCGenderNeutral-2) The French lyrics remain unaltered.

"O Canada" had served as a *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* national anthem since 1939, officially becoming the country's national anthem in 1980 when Canada's *National Anthem Act* received [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") and became effective on July 1 as part of that year's Dominion Day (today's [Canada Day](/wiki/Canada_Day "Canada Day")) celebrations.[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1)[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Melody

"O Canada" is a 28-[bar](/wiki/Bar_(music) "Bar (music)") song originally written in the key of F major for four voices and piano, as a [march](/wiki/March_(music) "March (music)") in [4/4 time](/wiki/4/4_time "4/4 time") to be played "*[maestoso](/wiki/Maestoso "Maestoso") e [risoluto](/wiki/Risoluto "Risoluto")*" ("majestic and resolved"). The original manuscript has been lost.[[4]](#cite_note-CE-4)

## Lyrics

The National Anthem Act established set lyrics for "O Canada" in Canada's two official languages, English and French. However, the two sets of lyrics are not translations of each other.

The lyrics are as follows:[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1)[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)

O Canada!  
Our home and native land!  
True patriot love in all of us command.  
With glowing hearts we see thee rise,  
The True North strong and free!  
From far and wide,   
O Canada, we stand on guard for thee.  
God keep our land glorious and free!  
𝄆 O Canada, we stand on guard for thee. 𝄇

Terre de nos aïeux,  
Ton front est ceint de fleurons glorieux!

The line "The True North strong and free" is based on [Lord Tennyson's](/wiki/Alfred,_Lord_Tennyson "Alfred, Lord Tennyson") description of Canada as "that true North, whereof we lately heard / A strain to shame us". In the context of Tennyson's poem *To the Queen*, the word *true* means "loyal" or "faithful".[[9]](#cite_note-colombo-10)

The lyrics and melody of "O Canada" are both in the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain"),[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1) a status unaffected by the trademarking of the phrases "with glowing hearts" and "*des plus brillants exploits*" for the [2010 Winter Olympics](/wiki/2010_Winter_Olympics "2010 Winter Olympics") in Vancouver.[[10]](#cite_note-11) Two provinces have adopted Latin translations of phrases from the English lyrics as their mottos: [Manitoba](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Manitoba "Coat of arms of Manitoba")—*Gloriosus et Liber* (Glorious and Free)[[11]](#cite_note-12)—and [Alberta](/wiki/Coat_of_arms_of_Alberta "Coat of arms of Alberta")—*Fortis et Liber* (Strong and Free).[[12]](#cite_note-13) Similarly, the [Canadian Army](/wiki/Canadian_Army "Canadian Army")'s motto is *Vigilamus pro te* (we stand on guard for thee).

### Translations

The lyrics have been translated into several [indigenous languages of Canada](/wiki/Indigenous_languages_of_Canada "Indigenous languages of Canada"), including [Inuktitut](/wiki/Inuktitut_language "Inuktitut language"), [Ojibwe](/wiki/Ojibwe_language "Ojibwe language"),[[13]](#cite_note-auto-14)[[14]](#cite_note-15) [Cree](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language")[[15]](#cite_note-16) and [Mi'kmaq](/wiki/Mi%27kmaq_language "Mi'kmaq language").[[16]](#cite_note-17) There is also a trilingual version, in English, French and Inuktitut.[[17]](#cite_note-18)

## History

The French lyrics of "O Canada" were written by Sir [Adolphe-Basile Routhier](/wiki/Adolphe-Basile_Routhier "Adolphe-Basile Routhier"), to music composed by [Calixa Lavallée](/wiki/Calixa_Lavall%C3%A9e "Calixa Lavallée"), as a [French Canadian](/wiki/French_Canadian "French Canadian") patriotic song for the [Saint-Jean-Baptiste Society](/wiki/Saint-Jean-Baptiste_Society "Saint-Jean-Baptiste Society") and first performed on June 24, 1880, at a [Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day](/wiki/Saint-Jean-Baptiste_Day "Saint-Jean-Baptiste Day") banquet in [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"). At that time, the "Chant National", also by Routhier, was popular amongst Francophones as an anthem,[[19]](#cite_note-QHE-20) while "[God Save the Queen](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen "God Save the Queen")" and "[The Maple Leaf Forever](/wiki/The_Maple_Leaf_Forever "The Maple Leaf Forever")" had, since 1867, been competing as unofficial national anthems in [English Canada](/wiki/English_Canada "English Canada"). "O Canada" joined that fray when a group of school children sang it for the 1901 tour of Canada by [the Duke and Duchess of Cornwall](/wiki/Duke_of_Cornwall "Duke of Cornwall") (later [King George V](/wiki/George_V "George V") and [Queen Mary](/wiki/Mary_of_Teck "Mary of Teck")).[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1) This was the first known performance of the song outside Quebec.[[20]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a2/%22O_Canada%21%22_sheet_music%2C_1906.png/250px-%22O_Canada%21%22_sheet_music%2C_1906.png)](/wiki/File:%22O_Canada!%22_sheet_music,_1906.png)

Sheet music for an English-language version of "O Canada" published in 1906

Five years later, the Whaley and Royce company in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto") published the music with the French text and a first translation into English by Thomas Bedford Richardson and, in 1908, *[Collier's Weekly](/wiki/Collier%27s "Collier's")* magazine held a competition to write new English lyrics for "O Canada". The competition was won by Mercy E. Powell McCulloch, but her version never gained wide acceptance.[[19]](#cite_note-QHE-20) In fact, many made English translations of Routhier's words; however, the most popular version was created in 1908 by Robert Stanley Weir, a lawyer and Recorder of the City of [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"). Weir's lyrics from 1908 contained no religious references and used the phrase "thou dost in us command" before they were changed by Weir in 1913 to read "in all thy sons command".[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1)[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[4]](#cite_note-CE-4) In 1926, a fourth verse of a religious nature was added.[[22]](#cite_note-Hansard2002-23) A slightly modified version was officially published for the Diamond Jubilee of Confederation in 1927, and gradually it became the most widely accepted and performed version of this song.[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1)

The tune was thought to have become the *[de facto](/wiki/De_facto "De facto")* national anthem after King [George VI](/wiki/George_VI "George VI") remained at attention during its playing at the dedication of the [National War Memorial](/wiki/National_War_Memorial_(Canada) "National War Memorial (Canada)") in [Ottawa](/wiki/Ottawa "Ottawa"), Ontario, on May 21, 1939;[[23]](#cite_note-24) though George was actually following a precedent set by his brother, [Edward](/wiki/Edward_VIII "Edward VIII"), the previous [king of Canada](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada"), when he dedicated the [Canadian National Vimy Memorial](/wiki/Canadian_National_Vimy_Memorial "Canadian National Vimy Memorial") in France in 1936.[[24]](#cite_note-Galbraith10-25) By-laws and practices governing the use of song during public events in municipalities varied; in Toronto, "God Save the King" or "God Save the Queen" was employed, while in Montreal it was "O Canada".

Musicologist [Ross Duffin](/wiki/Ross_W._Duffin "Ross W. Duffin") has argued that Lavallée constructed the melody for "O Canada" by adapting material by Mozart ("[March of the Priests](/wiki/The_Magic_Flute "The Magic Flute")", measures 1–8), Wagner ("Wach auf, es nahet gen den Tag", measures 9–16), Liszt ("Festklänge", measures 17–20), and Matthias Keller ("The American Hymn", measures 21–28).[[25]](#cite_note-26)[[26]](#cite_note-27)

### Adoption

Prime Minister [Lester B. Pearson](/wiki/Lester_B._Pearson "Lester B. Pearson") in 1964 said one song would have to be chosen as the country's national anthem and the government resolved to form a joint committee to review the status of the two musical works. The next year, Pearson put to the House of Commons a motion that "the government be authorized to take such steps as may be necessary to provide that 'O Canada' shall be the National Anthem of Canada while 'God Save the Queen' shall be the Royal Anthem of Canada", of which parliament approved. In 1967, the Prime Minister advised [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") [Georges Vanier](/wiki/Georges_Vanier "Georges Vanier") to appoint the Special Joint Committee of the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") and [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada") on the National and Royal Anthems; the group first met in February and,[[4]](#cite_note-CE-4) within two months, on April 12, 1967, presented its conclusion that "O Canada" should be designated as the national anthem and "[God Save the Queen](/wiki/God_Save_the_Queen "God Save the Queen")" as the [royal anthem](/wiki/Honors_music "Honors music") of Canada,[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1) one verse from each, in [both official languages](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada"), to be adopted by parliament. The group was then charged with establishing official lyrics for each song. For "O Canada", the Robert Stanley Weir version of 1908 was recommended for the English words, with a few minor changes: two of the "stand on guard" phrases were replaced with "from far and wide" and "God keep our land".[[27]](#cite_note-28)[[1]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada-1)

In 1970, the [Queen in Right of Canada](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Canada "Monarchy of Canada") purchased the right to the lyrics and music of "O Canada" from Gordon V. Thompson Music for $1.[[28]](#cite_note-29) The song finally became the official national anthem in 1980 with the passage of the *National Anthem Act.*[[24]](#cite_note-Galbraith10-25)[[4]](#cite_note-CE-4) The Act replaced two of the repetitions of the phrase "We stand on guard" in the English lyrics, as had been proposed by the Senate Special Joint Committee. This change was controversial with traditionalists and, for several years afterwards, it was not uncommon to hear people still singing the old lyrics at public events. In contrast, the French lyrics are unchanged from the original version.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

### Inclusive language debates

In June 1990, [Toronto City Council](/wiki/Toronto_City_Council "Toronto City Council") voted 12 to 7 in favour of recommending to the [Canadian government](/wiki/Government_of_Canada "Government of Canada") that the phrase "our home and native land" be changed to "our home and cherished land" and that "in all thy sons command" be partly reverted to "in all of us command". Councillor [Howard Moscoe](/wiki/Howard_Moscoe "Howard Moscoe") said that the words "native land" were not appropriate for the many Canadians who were not native-born and that the word "sons" implied "that women can't feel true patriotism or love for Canada".[[30]](#cite_note-31) [Senator](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") [Vivienne Poy](/wiki/Vivienne_Poy "Vivienne Poy") similarly criticized the English lyrics of the anthem as being [sexist](/wiki/Gender-neutral_language "Gender-neutral language") and she introduced a bill in 2002 proposing to change the phrase "in all thy sons command" to "in all of us command".[[22]](#cite_note-Hansard2002-23) In the late 2000s, the anthem's religious references (to God in English and to the [Christian cross](/wiki/Christian_cross "Christian cross") in French) were criticized by [secularists](/wiki/Secularist "Secularist").[[31]](#cite_note-32)

In the [speech from the throne](/wiki/Speech_from_the_throne "Speech from the throne") delivered by [Governor General](/wiki/Governor_General_of_Canada "Governor General of Canada") [Michaëlle Jean](/wiki/Micha%C3%ABlle_Jean "Michaëlle Jean") on March 3, 2010, a plan to have parliament review the "original [gender-neutral](/wiki/Gender-neutral "Gender-neutral") wording of the national anthem" was announced.[[32]](#cite_note-33) However, three-quarters of Canadians polled after the speech objected to the proposal and,[[33]](#cite_note-34) two days later, the [prime minister's office](/wiki/Office_of_the_Prime_Minister_(Canada) "Office of the Prime Minister (Canada)") announced that the [cabinet](/wiki/Cabinet_of_Canada "Cabinet of Canada") had decided not to change the original lyrics.[[34]](#cite_note-35)

In another attempt to make the anthem gender-neutral, Liberal MP [Mauril Bélanger](/wiki/Mauril_B%C3%A9langer "Mauril Bélanger") introduced a [private member's bill](/wiki/Private_member%27s_bill "Private member's bill") in September 2014. His Bill C-624, *An Act to amend the National Anthem Act (gender)*, was defeated at second reading in April 2015.[[35]](#cite_note-36) Following the [2015 federal election](/wiki/2015_Canadian_federal_election "2015 Canadian federal election"), Bélanger reintroduced the bill in the new parliament as Bill C-210 in January 2016.[[36]](#cite_note-C-210-37) In June 2016, the bill passed its third reading with a vote of 225 to 74 in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_Canada "House of Commons of Canada").[[37]](#cite_note-38) The bill passed its third reading in the [Senate](/wiki/Senate_of_Canada "Senate of Canada") with a [voice vote](/wiki/Voice_vote "Voice vote") on January 31, 2018, and received [royal assent](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") on February 7, 2018.[[38]](#cite_note-passed_in_senate-39)[[39]](#cite_note-40)

## Performances

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Lyndon_Slewidge_singing.JPG/250px-Lyndon_Slewidge_singing.JPG)](/wiki/File:Lyndon_Slewidge_singing.JPG)

[Lyndon Slewidge](/wiki/Lyndon_Slewidge "Lyndon Slewidge") singing "O Canada" prior to a [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators") game

"O Canada" is routinely played before sporting events involving Canadian teams. Singers at such public events often mix the English and French lyrics to represent [Canada's linguistic duality](/wiki/Official_bilingualism_in_Canada "Official bilingualism in Canada").[[40]](#cite_note-41) Other linguistic variations have also been performed: During the opening ceremonies of the [1988 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1988_Winter_Olympics "1988 Winter Olympics") in Calgary, "O Canada" was sung in the [Southern Tutchone language](/wiki/Tutchone_language "Tutchone language") by Yukon native Daniel Tlen.[[41]](#cite_note-42)[[42]](#cite_note-43) At a [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League") (NHL) game in Calgary, in February 2007, [Cree](/wiki/Cree "Cree") singer [Akina Shirt](/wiki/Akina_Shirt "Akina Shirt") became the first person to perform "O Canada" in the [Cree language](/wiki/Cree_language "Cree language") at such an event.[[43]](#cite_note-44)

[Major League Baseball](/wiki/Major_League_Baseball "Major League Baseball"), [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer"), the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association"), and the NHL all require venues to perform both the Canadian and [American national anthems](/wiki/The_Star-Spangled_Banner "The Star-Spangled Banner") at games that involve teams from both countries (including [all-star games](/wiki/All-star_game "All-star game")), with the away team's anthem being performed first, followed by the host country.[[44]](#cite_note-45) The NHL's [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") play both anthems before every home game, regardless of the opponent, in recognition of the team's significant Canadian fanbase.[[45]](#cite_note-46) Baseball teams play "O Canada" at games involving the [Toronto Blue Jays](/wiki/Toronto_Blue_Jays "Toronto Blue Jays")[[46]](#cite_note-47) and their [High-A](/wiki/High-A "High-A") [Minor League Baseball](/wiki/Minor_League_Baseball "Minor League Baseball") affiliate, the [Vancouver Canadians](/wiki/Vancouver_Canadians "Vancouver Canadians"),[[47]](#cite_note-48) [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") teams do so for games involving the [Toronto Raptors](/wiki/Toronto_Raptors "Toronto Raptors"), and [Major League Soccer](/wiki/Major_League_Soccer "Major League Soccer") teams do so for matches involving [Toronto FC](/wiki/Toronto_FC "Toronto FC"), [CF Montréal](/wiki/CF_Montr%C3%A9al "CF Montréal"), and [Vancouver Whitecaps FC](/wiki/Vancouver_Whitecaps_FC "Vancouver Whitecaps FC"). In recognition of their Canadian fanbases and [Buffalo](/wiki/Buffalo,_New_York "Buffalo, New York")'s proximity to Canada, the [Buffalo Bills](/wiki/Buffalo_Bills "Buffalo Bills") of the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") and the [Buffalo Bisons](/wiki/Buffalo_Bisons "Buffalo Bisons"), the MiLB [Triple-A](/wiki/Triple-A_(baseball) "Triple-A (baseball)") affiliate of the Toronto Blue Jays, play "O Canada" before "The Star-Spangled Banner" at their home games. "O Canada" has also been performed during NHL Global Series games involving Canadian teams as well as NBA Global Games involving the Raptors and MLB World Tour games involving the Blue Jays.

## Laws and etiquette

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d6/Stained_glass%2C_Oh_Canada_Royal_Military_College_of_Canada_Club_Montreal_1965.jpg/250px-Stained_glass%2C_Oh_Canada_Royal_Military_College_of_Canada_Club_Montreal_1965.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stained_glass,_Oh_Canada_Royal_Military_College_of_Canada_Club_Montreal_1965.jpg)

A portion of "O Canada"'s lyrics in stained glass at the [Royal Military College of Canada](/wiki/Royal_Military_College_of_Canada "Royal Military College of Canada")

The *National Anthem Act* specifies the lyrics and melody of "O Canada", placing both of them in the [public domain](/wiki/Public_domain "Public domain"), allowing the anthem to be freely reproduced or used as a base for derived works, including musical arrangements.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50) There are no regulations governing the performance of "O Canada", leaving citizens to exercise their best judgment. When it is performed at an event, traditional etiquette is to either start or end the ceremonies with the anthem, including situations when other anthems are played and for the audience to stand during the performance. Civilian men usually remove their hats, while women and children are not required to do so.[[50]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada2-51) Military men and women in uniform traditionally keep their hats on and offer the military salute during the performance of the anthem, with the salute offered in the direction of the [Canadian Flag](/wiki/Flag_of_Canada "Flag of Canada") if one is present, and if not present it is offered standing at attention.[[50]](#cite_note-DCHOCanada2-51)

Presently, provincial regulations and policies in Manitoba, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Ontario and Prince Edward Island mandate the national anthem to be played daily in public elementary and secondary schools.[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-reg-53) "O Canada" is to be played in British Columbia schools at least three times a year at assemblies.[[52]](#cite_note-reg-53) Other provinces and territories do not have provincially-mandated regulations and policies for playing the national anthem in schools.[[52]](#cite_note-reg-53)

## Adaptations

In the 1950s, the melody of "O Canada" was adapted for the school anthem of the [Ateneo de Manila University](/wiki/Ateneo_de_Manila_University "Ateneo de Manila University"). Titled "[A Song for Mary](/wiki/Culture_and_traditions_of_the_Ateneo_de_Manila#A_Song_for_Mary "Culture and traditions of the Ateneo de Manila")" or simply "The Ateneo de Manila Graduation Hymn", the lyrics were written by [James B. Reuter](/wiki/James_B._Reuter "James B. Reuter"), [SJ](/wiki/Society_of_Jesus "Society of Jesus"), and the original tune adapted by Col. José Manela Campaña, bandmaster of the defunct [Philippine Constabulary Band](/wiki/Philippine_Constabulary_Band "Philippine Constabulary Band").[[53]](#cite_note-54)[[54]](#cite_note-55)
